title: Fitting push-fit plastic waste pipe
subtitle: Cut, deburr, push the fittings home, and test for watertightness
excerpt: How to extend or replace a plastic waste pipe run using push-fit fittings: cutting 32 mm or 40 mm solvent-weld or push-fit waste pipe, fitting connectors and traps, and checking the fall on the run.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Wavin, FloPlast technical installation guides (publicly available). WRAS approved fittings guidance.

content.text: Plastic waste pipe extends runs, replaces failed sections, or connects new appliances to the soil stack. Push-fit fittings (marked in grey or white plastic) require no solvent cement or heat tool; the pipe pushes into the fitting O-ring and the joint is complete. Solvent-weld fittings (the older type) require cleaning fluid and solvent cement applied to the pipe and fitting before pushing together; they set permanently in 5 to 10 minutes.
What you need
Plastic waste pipe, 32 mm or 40 mm.
32 mm is standard for basins; 40 mm for baths, showers, and kitchen sinks. Sold in 3 m lengths.
Push-fit or solvent-weld fittings.
Straight couplers, 90-degree elbows, 45-degree elbows, tee connectors, and pipe clips as needed for the run.
P-trap or bottle trap.
Fits below the appliance waste outlet. Push-fit type for easy removal.
Fine-toothed saw or pipe-shears.
For cutting waste pipe cleanly.
Step-by-step
Step 1, plan the run and check the fall.
Waste pipes need a minimum fall of 18 mm per metre of run (about 1 in 50) to drain by gravity. Too shallow and the pipe siphons the
trap
or silts up. Too steep and the water runs faster than the solids, leaving them behind. Use the spirit level and tape to check the fall before cutting pipe.
Step 2, cut the pipe.

Mark the cut with a pencil line square around the pipe. Hold the pipe in a vice or against a bench stop. Cut with a fine-toothed saw: a coarse saw tears the plastic and leaves a rough edge that leaks. The cut must be square: an angled cut prevents the pipe seating fully in the fitting.
Step 3, deburr the cut end.

Remove the burr from the cut edge with a file or deburring tool. On push-fit fittings, the O-ring is easily damaged by a sharp burr; a scored O-ring will never seal. Chamfer the outer edge of the pipe end slightly; this makes it easier to push the pipe into the fitting.
Step 4, push the fitting.
Push the prepared pipe end firmly into the
push-fit fitting

until it reaches the depth mark on the pipe or hits the internal stop inside the fitting. Mark the insertion depth on the pipe with a pencil before pushing: if the mark remains visible after insertion, the pipe has not been pushed fully home and the joint will leak.
What it looks like when it is right

Run water through the completed trap and waste run. Check each fitting for drips. With a push-fit run, a correctly inserted pipe cannot be pulled out without releasing the fitting's internal grab ring (or cutting the pipe). If the pipe can be pulled out with hand pressure, it is not fully home.
Common mistakes
Insufficient fall.
A flat run pools water and odours. Check the fall with a spirit level at every pipe clip position before fixing.
Pipe not pushed fully into the fitting.
Mark the insertion depth on the pipe and confirm the mark is hidden after insertion.
Too many 90-degree bends in one run.
Each 90-degree bend reduces flow significantly. Limit changes of direction; use 45-degree bends where possible and keep horizontal runs as short and direct as feasible.
